question_frequency.rationale: The Pair offered three explicit decision points (traversal direction, copy vs in-place, path reconstruction strategy), engaging the user at each meaningful fork. Could have surfaced more (e.g., tiebreakers, edge cases) but the cadence is solidly didactic.

question_quality.rationale: The bottom-up vs top-down and greedy-trace vs recorded-choices forks hit real algorithmic trade-offs. The copy vs in-place choice is more cosmetic but still substantive. None reach the deepest issue (e.g., correctness of greedy backtrack), but they're consequential.

scaffolding.rationale: Logical progression: choose direction → choose memory model → solve → choose reconstruction strategy. Difficulty rises smoothly without spoilers; option descriptions are neutral. Slight missed opportunity to probe why the greedy trace actually works.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Natural back-and-forth with concise affirmations and clear next steps. The structured OPTIONS format is clean if a bit mechanical, but conversation reads well and stays actionable.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code correctly computes max sum (62) and reconstructs the path on a 10-row triangle, equivalent to baseline. Baseline used the actual PE #67 numbers and added a cross-check sum; experiment used a smaller invented triangle, but meets the stated requirements.

friction.rationale: Took 4 turns and ~2x the wall time of baseline, but the extra length delivered three genuine decision points and pedagogical payoff proportional to the overhead.
